Stat 361 — Recitation 5

Serenay Çakar

2026-26-03

Background Theory

Transformation Methods

Many probability distributions can be generated by applying a deterministic function to one or more random variables from a known (easy-to-sample) distribution. This avoids direct numerical inversion of complex CDFs.

Key Transformation Reference Table

Input Distribution(s) Transformation Result
\(Z \sim N(0,1)\) \(Z^2\) \(\chi^2_1\)
\(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,1)\) \(\sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2\) \(\chi^2_n\)
\(U \sim \chi^2_m,\; V \sim \chi^2_n\) indep. \(F = \dfrac{U/m}{V/n}\) \(F(m,n)\)
\(Z \sim N(0,1),\; V \sim \chi^2_n\) indep. \(T = \dfrac{Z}{\sqrt{V/n}}\) \(t_n\)
\(U_i \overset{iid}{\sim} \text{Exp}(\theta)\) \(\sum_{i=1}^{t} U_i\) \(\text{Gamma}(t,\theta)\)
\(X, Y \sim \text{Exp}(1)\) indep. \(Z = \mu - s\log(X/Y)\) \(\text{Logistic}(\mu,s)\)
\(X \sim F(n,m)\) \(Z = \dfrac{\log(X)}{2}\) \(\text{Fisher's Z}(n,m)\)
\(X \sim \text{Weibull}(\lambda, \tfrac{1}{2})\) \(Y = \sqrt{X}\) \(\text{Exponential}(1/\sqrt{\lambda})\)
\(U \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\) \(V = \sum_{i=1}^{t} U_i\) \(\text{Erlang}(t,\lambda)\)
\(U \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda),\; V = \sum U_i\) \(Z = U/V + 1\) \(\text{Pareto}(1,t)\)
\(U, V \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)\) indep. (Box–Muller) \(Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\log U}\cos(2\pi V),\quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\log U}\sin(2\pi V)\) \(Z_1, Z_2 \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,1)\)
\(U \sim \Gamma(r,\lambda),\; V \sim \Gamma(s,\lambda)\) indep. \(X = \dfrac{U}{U+V}\) \(\text{Beta}(r,s)\)
\(U, V \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)\) indep. \(X = \left\lfloor 1 + \dfrac{\log(V)}{\log(1-(1-\theta)^U)} \right\rfloor\) \(\text{Logarithmic}(\theta)\)
\(Y \sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) \(X = e^Y\) \(\text{Lognormal}(\mu,\sigma^2)\)

Convolution vs. Mixture

Convolution (Sum): \(S = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n\)

  • Result is unimodal with increased variance: \(E(S) = nE(X)\), \(\text{Var}(S) = n\,\text{Var}(X)\)
  • Simulate by generating \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) independently and summing

Mixture: Each observation drawn from distribution \(F_j\) with probability \(p_j\)

  • \(F_X = p_1 F_{X_1} + p_2 F_{X_2} + \cdots\)
  • Often bimodal — preserves distinct component shapes
  • Simulate by drawing \(U \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)\), then choosing which component to sample from
set.seed(361)
n <- 1000

x1 <- rnorm(n, mean = 0, sd = 1)   # N(0,1)
x2 <- rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 1)   # N(5,1)

# Convolution: sum the two variables
s <- x1 + x2   # should be N(5, 2)

# Mixture: pick from x1 or x2 with prob 0.5 each
u <- runif(n)
k <- as.integer(u > 0.5)
x <- k * x1 + (1 - k) * x2

par(mfcol = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

hist(s, prob = TRUE, main = "Convolution: X1 + X2",
     xlab = "Value", col = "#AED6F1", border = "white", breaks = 30)
curve(dnorm(x, 5, sqrt(2)), col = "navy", lwd = 2, add = TRUE)

hist(x, prob = TRUE, main = "Mixture: 0.5·N(0,1) + 0.5·N(5,1)",
     xlab = "Value", col = "#A9DFBF", border = "white", breaks = 30)
xs <- seq(-4, 9, 0.01)
lines(xs, 0.5*dnorm(xs,0,1) + 0.5*dnorm(xs,5,1), col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)

par(mfcol = c(1,1))

Key difference: The convolution produces a single bell curve centred at the sum of means. The mixture retains the two separate modes — classic bimodal shape.

Question 1 — Gamma(4, 9) via Exponential Convolution

Write a function to generate \(n = 10{,}000\) random variables from \(\text{Gamma}(4, 9)\) using its relationship with the Exponential distribution. Visualize with a histogram and theoretical density. Compare sample mean and variance with theoretical values.

Theory

Key relationship: Since \(t = 4\) is a positive integer: \[\sum_{i=1}^{4} \text{Exp}(\lambda) \sim \text{Gamma}(4,\,\lambda)\]

Inverse transform for Exponential: \[X_i = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log(U_i), \quad U_i \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)\]

So generating one \(\text{Gamma}(4, 9)\) variate requires summing 4 independent \(\text{Exp}(9)\) variates: \[Y = X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + X_4\]

Theoretical moments: \(E(X) = t/\lambda = 4/9 \approx 0.444\), \(\text{Var}(X) = t/\lambda^2 = 4/81 \approx 0.049\)

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# gamma_simulation: generates n samples from Gamma(t, lambda)
#   using the convolution of t independent Exp(lambda) r.v.s
#   Each Exp(lambda) variate is produced by the inverse transform:
#   X = -log(U) / lambda,  U ~ Unif(0,1)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
gamma_simulation <- function(n, t, lambda) {
  # Step 1: Generate a t x n matrix of Uniform(0,1) samples
  U <- matrix(runif(n * t), nrow = t, ncol = n)
  # Step 2: Apply ITM to transform each uniform to Exp(lambda)
  X <- -log(U) / lambda
  # Step 3: Sum down each column → one Gamma(t, lambda) per column
  Y <- colSums(X)
  return(Y)
}

# Parameters
n      <- 1e4
t      <- 4
lambda <- 9

Y <- gamma_simulation(n, t, lambda)

# Plot
hist(Y, probability = TRUE,
     main  = "Gamma(4, 9) via Convolution of 4 × Exp(9)",
     xlab  = "Y", ylab = "Density",
     col   = "#AED6F1", border = "white", breaks = 40)
curve(dgamma(x, shape = t, rate = lambda),
      col = "navy", lwd = 2.5, add = TRUE)
legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical Gamma(4,9)"),
       fill = c("#AED6F1", NA), lty = c(NA,1),
       lwd = c(NA,2.5), col = c("black","navy"), bty = "n")

# Moment comparison
results_q1 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(t/lambda,       mean(Y)), 5),
  Variance = round(c(t/lambda^2,     var(Y)),  5),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q1, caption = "Gamma(4,9): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Gamma(4,9): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 0.44444 0.04938
Estimated 0.44201 0.04864

Interpretation: The histogram fits the theoretical \(\text{Gamma}(4,9)\) density curve closely. Both the sample mean (\(\approx 0.442\)) and variance (\(\approx 0.049\)) match the theoretical values (\(0.444\) and \(0.049\)) — confirming the simulation is correct. By the SLLN, \(\bar{Y} \xrightarrow{a.s.} E(Y)\) as \(n \to \infty\).

Question 2 — Chi-Square(5) via Squared Normals

Write a function to generate \(n = 10{,}000\) samples from \(\chi^2_5\) using its relationship with the standard normal distribution. Visualize and compare estimated moments with theoretical values.

Theory

Key relationship: \[Z_i \overset{iid}{\sim} N(0,1) \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{v} Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_v\]

Each \(Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_1\) (a Gamma(1/2, 1/2) distribution). By the reproductive property of the Gamma distribution, the sum of \(v\) independent \(\chi^2_1\) variables is \(\chi^2_v\).

Theoretical moments: \(E(X) = v = 5\), \(\text{Var}(X) = 2v = 10\)

Algorithm: 1. Generate \(v \times n\) standard normals 2. Square each value 3. Sum across the \(v\) rows for each of the \(n\) observations

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# chisq_simulation: generates n samples from chi-square(v)
#   by summing v squared N(0,1) random variables
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
chisq_simulation <- function(n, v) {
  # Generate a v x n matrix of standard normals
  Z <- matrix(rnorm(n * v, 0, 1), nrow = v, ncol = n)
  # Sum of squared columns → chi-square(v) per column
  X <- colSums(Z^2)
  return(X)
}

n <- 10000
v <- 5
X <- chisq_simulation(n, v)

# Plot
hist(X, probability = TRUE,
     main  = expression(paste(chi^2, "(v = 5) via Sum of Squared N(0,1)")),
     xlab  = "X", ylab = "Density",
     col   = "#A9DFBF", border = "white", breaks = 40, ylim = c(0, 0.20))
curve(dchisq(x, df = v), col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2.5, add = TRUE)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Simulated", expression(paste("Theoretical ", chi^2, "(5)"))),
       fill = c("#A9DFBF",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","darkgreen"), bty="n")

# Moment comparison
results_q2 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(v,     mean(X)), 5),
  Variance = round(c(2*v,   var(X)),  5),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q2, caption = "Chi-Square(5): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Chi-Square(5): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 5.00000 10.00000
Estimated 4.99608 9.93858

Interpretation: Each \(Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_1\) contributes \(E = 1\), \(\text{Var} = 2\). By independence, summing 5 of them gives \(E = 5\), \(\text{Var} = 10\) — both confirmed by the simulation. Note that \(\chi^2_v \equiv \text{Gamma}(v/2,\, 1/2)\), so this is a special case of Question 1.

Question 3 — Student-t(25) via Normal/Chi-Square Ratio

Write a function to generate \(n = 10{,}000\) samples from \(t_{25}\) using a transformation method. Visualize with the theoretical density.

Theory

Key transformation: \[T = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/k}}, \quad Z \sim N(0,1),\; V \sim \chi^2_k \text{ (independent)} \;\Rightarrow\; T \sim t_k\]

Theoretical moments (for \(k > 2\)): \[E(T) = 0, \qquad \text{Var}(T) = \frac{k}{k-2} = \frac{25}{23} \approx 1.087\]

The \(t\) distribution has heavier tails than \(N(0,1)\) — as \(k \to \infty\), \(t_k \to N(0,1)\).

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# tdist_simulation: generates n samples from t(k)
#   using T = Z / sqrt(V/k), where Z ~ N(0,1) and V ~ chi-sq(k)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
tdist_simulation <- function(n, k) {
  # Numerator: n standard normals
  Z_num <- rnorm(n)
  # Denominator: generate V ~ chi-square(k) as sum of k squared normals
  Z_mat <- matrix(rnorm(n * k), nrow = n, ncol = k)
  V     <- rowSums(Z_mat^2)
  # T-distributed values
  T_vals <- Z_num / sqrt(V / k)
  return(T_vals)
}

n <- 10000
k <- 25
T_values <- tdist_simulation(n, k)

# Plot
hist(T_values, probability = TRUE,
     main = "Student-t (df = 25) via Transformation",
     xlab = "T", ylab = "Density",
     col = "#F9E4B7", border = "white", breaks = 50, ylim = c(0, 0.42))
curve(dt(x, df = k),   col = "darkorange", lwd = 2.5, add = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x),        col = "steelblue",  lwd = 1.5, lty = 2, add = TRUE)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical t(25)", "N(0,1) reference"),
       fill   = c("#F9E4B7", NA, NA),
       lty    = c(NA, 1, 2), lwd = c(NA, 2.5, 1.5),
       col    = c("black","darkorange","steelblue"), bty = "n")

# Moment comparison
results_q3 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(0,         mean(T_values)), 4),
  Variance = round(c(k/(k-2),   var(T_values)),  4),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q3, caption = "t(25): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
t(25): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 0.000 1.0870
Estimated 0.011 1.1191

Interpretation: The \(t_{25}\) distribution is symmetric around 0 (confirmed by sample mean \(\approx 0\)) but slightly heavier-tailed than \(N(0,1)\) — visible in the comparison with the dashed blue curve. The variance (\(\approx 1.087\)) exceeds 1, reflecting those heavier tails.

Question 4 — Pareto(1, 18) via Erlang Transformation

Generate \(n = 10^5\) samples from \(\text{Pareto}(1, 18)\) using the relationship between Exponential(8), Erlang, and Pareto distributions. Use only runif() to generate random numbers. Verify the sample has Pareto(1, 18) properties.

Theory

Chain of transformations:

Step 1 — Exponential via ITM: \[X_i = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log U_i, \quad U_i \sim \text{Unif}(0,1) \;\Rightarrow\; X_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\]

Step 2 — Erlang via convolution: \[V = \sum_{i=1}^{t} X_i \sim \text{Erlang}(t, \lambda)\]

Step 3 — Pareto via ratio: \[Z = \frac{U}{V} + 1 \sim \text{Pareto}(1, t)\]

For our case: \(\lambda = 8\), \(t = 18\), so we generate \(V \sim \text{Erlang}(18, 8)\) and compute \(Z = U/V + 1 \sim \text{Pareto}(1, 18)\) where \(U \sim \text{Exp}(8)\) is one of the 18 Exponential variates.

Pareto PDF: \(f(x) = \alpha\beta^\alpha / x^{\alpha+1}\), for \(x > \beta\)

Moments: \(E(X) = t/(t-1) = 18/17 \approx 1.059\) (for \(t > 1\)), \(\text{Var}(X) = t/[(t-1)^2(t-2)] = 18/(17^2 \times 16) \approx 0.00389\)

Solution

# install.packages("sads")  
library(sads)   # provides dpareto()

set.seed(361)

n      <- 1e5
t      <- 18
lambda <- 8

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Generate a t x n matrix of Uniform(0,1) values
#         Each column produces t Exp(lambda) variates via ITM
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
U <- matrix(runif(n * t), nrow = t, ncol = n)

# Step 2: Apply ITM → each entry becomes Exp(lambda)
exponential <- -log(U) / lambda

# Step 3: Column sums → Erlang(t, lambda) per sample
#         erlang[j] = sum of t Exp(lambda) values = V
erlang <- colSums(exponential)

# Step 4: Use the first row as the U ~ Exp(lambda) numerator
#         Z = U/V + 1 ~ Pareto(1, t)
# Note: exponential[1,] is one Exp(lambda) row (independent of erlang
#       only if we use a separate draw — here we use a fresh row)
U_num  <- -log(runif(n)) / lambda   # fresh Exp(lambda) for numerator
pareto <- (U_num / erlang) + 1

# Plot
hist(pareto, prob = TRUE, breaks = 100,
     xlim = c(min(pareto), quantile(pareto, 0.999)),
     main = "Pareto(1, 18) via Erlang Transformation",
     xlab = "X", col = "#D7BDE2", border = "white")
y_seq <- seq(1, quantile(pareto, 0.999), 0.001)
lines(y_seq, dpareto(y_seq, scale = 1, shape = t),
      col = "purple", lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical Pareto(1,18)"),
       fill = c("#D7BDE2",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","purple"), bty="n")

# Moment verification
E_pareto   <- t / (t - 1)
Var_pareto <- t / ((t-1)^2 * (t-2))

results_q4 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(E_pareto,   mean(pareto)), 5),
  Variance = round(c(Var_pareto, var(pareto)),  5),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q4, caption = "Pareto(1,18): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Pareto(1,18): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 1.05882 0.00389
Estimated 1.05896 0.00393

Interpretation: The Pareto distribution has a very heavy right tail — most mass is near the lower bound of 1. The histogram fits the theoretical Pareto(1,18) density well. The estimated mean is close to \(18/17 \approx 1.059\). Only runif() was used at the random-number generation stage, with the Exponential-Erlang-Pareto chain providing the full transformation.

Question 5 — Logistic(5, 7) via Exponential Ratio

Generate \(n = 10^5\) samples from \(\text{Logistic}(5, 7)\) using the transformation method based on two independent \(\text{Exp}(1)\) random variables. Compare histogram with theoretical density.

Theory

Key transformation: \[X \sim \text{Exp}(1),\quad Y \sim \text{Exp}(1) \text{ independent} \;\Rightarrow\; Z = \mu - s\log\!\left(\frac{X}{Y}\right) \sim \text{Logistic}(\mu, s)\]

Inverse transform for Exponential: \[X = -\frac{1}{\lambda}\log U, \quad U \sim \text{Unif}(0,1)\]

Logistic PDF: \[f(x) = \frac{e^{-(x-\mu)/s}}{s\left(1 + e^{-(x-\mu)/s}\right)^2}\]

Moments: \(E(Z) = \mu = 5\), \(\text{Var}(Z) = s^2\pi^2/3 = 49\pi^2/3 \approx 161.1\)

Solution

set.seed(361)

n      <- 1e5
lambda <- 1
mu     <- 5
s      <- 7

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Generate two independent Exp(1) variates via ITM
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
u1 <- runif(n)
x  <- -log(u1) / lambda   # X ~ Exp(1)

u2 <- runif(n)
y  <- -log(u2) / lambda   # Y ~ Exp(1)

# Apply the Logistic transformation
logistic_sample <- mu - s * log(x / y)

# Plot
hist(logistic_sample, prob = TRUE,
     main = "Logistic(5, 7) via Exponential Ratio Transformation",
     xlab = "Z", col = "#FDEBD0", border = "white",
     breaks = 80, xlim = c(-60, 70))

# Overlay theoretical Logistic PDF
a_seq <- seq(-80, 90, 0.1)
logistic_pdf <- exp(-(a_seq - mu)/s) / (s * (1 + exp(-(a_seq - mu)/s))^2)
lines(a_seq, logistic_pdf, col = "red", lwd = 2.5)

legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical Logistic(5,7)"),
       fill = c("#FDEBD0",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","red"), bty="n")

# Moment comparison
results_q5 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(mu,               mean(logistic_sample)), 4),
  Variance = round(c(s^2 * pi^2 / 3,   var(logistic_sample)),  4),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q5, caption = "Logistic(5,7): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Logistic(5,7): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 5.0000 161.2035
Estimated 4.9748 161.9384

Interpretation: The Logistic distribution is symmetric around \(\mu = 5\) with heavier tails than the Normal. The sample mean closely matches \(\mu = 5\). The large variance (\(\approx 161\)) reflects the wide, heavy-tailed shape — note the x-axis extends well beyond \(\pm 30\). The theoretical density (red curve) fits the histogram well across the entire range.

Question 6 — F(2, 8) via Chi-Square Ratio

Generate \(n = 10^5\) samples from \(F(2, 8)\) using the appropriate transformation. Compare histogram with the theoretical \(F\) density.

Theory

Key transformation: \[U \sim \chi^2_m, \quad V \sim \chi^2_n \text{ (independent)} \;\Rightarrow\; F = \frac{U/m}{V/n} \sim F(m, n)\]

We generate each \(\chi^2\) variable as the sum of squared standard normals (from Question 2).

Theoretical moments (for \(n > 4\)): \[E(F) = \frac{n}{n-2} = \frac{8}{6} \approx 1.333, \qquad \text{Var}(F) = \frac{2n^2(m+n-2)}{m(n-2)^2(n-4)} = \frac{2 \cdot 64 \cdot 8}{2 \cdot 36 \cdot 4} = \frac{1024}{288} \approx 3.556\]

Solution

set.seed(361)

N <- 1e5
m <- 2
n <- 8

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Generate U ~ chi-square(m) and V ~ chi-square(n) independently
# U = sum of m squared N(0,1) values (one per column)
# V = sum of n squared N(0,1) values (one per column)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
z1         <- matrix(rnorm(m * N), nrow = m, ncol = N)
z2         <- matrix(rnorm(n * N), nrow = n, ncol = N)
U          <- colSums(z1^2)   # U ~ chi-square(m=2)
V          <- colSums(z2^2)   # V ~ chi-square(n=8)
Rv_F       <- (U / m) / (V / n)   # F(m,n) = F(2,8)

# Plot (truncate right tail for readability)
x_lim <- quantile(Rv_F, 0.995)
hist(Rv_F, breaks = "FD", prob = TRUE,
     main  = "F(2, 8) via Chi-Square Ratio",
     xlab  = "F", col = "#D2B4DE", border = "white",
     xlim  = c(0, x_lim))
a_seq <- seq(0.001, x_lim, 0.01)
lines(a_seq, df(a_seq, df1 = m, df2 = n), col = "red", lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical F(2,8)"),
       fill = c("#D2B4DE",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","red"), bty="n")

# Moment comparison
E_F   <- n / (n - 2)
Var_F <- (2 * n^2 * (m + n - 2)) / (m * (n-2)^2 * (n-4))

results_q6 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(E_F,   mean(Rv_F)), 4),
  Variance = round(c(Var_F, var(Rv_F)),  4),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q6, caption = "F(2,8): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
F(2,8): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 1.3333 3.5556
Estimated 1.3348 3.7636

Interpretation: The \(F(2,8)\) distribution is right-skewed and defined on \((0, \infty)\). The mean exceeds 1 (\(\approx 1.333\)) and the variance is large (\(\approx 3.556\)) due to the heavy right tail. The red theoretical density line closely tracks the simulated histogram. Note: the x-axis is truncated at the 99.5th percentile for visual clarity, as a few extreme outliers are expected.

Question 7 — Beta(3, 5) via Gamma Ratio

Write a function to generate \(n = 10{,}000\) samples from \(\text{Beta}(3, 5)\) using the Gamma-to-Beta relationship, with \(\lambda = 6\). Check the histogram, add theoretical density, and compare moments.

Theory

Key transformation: \[U \sim \text{Gamma}(r, \lambda), \quad V \sim \text{Gamma}(s, \lambda) \text{ (independent)} \;\Rightarrow\; X = \frac{U}{U+V} \sim \text{Beta}(r, s)\]

We build each Gamma from Exponential variates via ITM: \[G_1 = \sum_{i=1}^{3} E_i \sim \text{Gamma}(3, 6), \quad G_2 = \sum_{i=1}^{5} E_i \sim \text{Gamma}(5, 6)\] where each \(E_i = -\frac{1}{6}\log U_i\).

Then: \(\text{Beta} = G_1 / (G_1 + G_2) \sim \text{Beta}(3, 5)\)

Theoretical moments: \[E(X) = \frac{r}{r+s} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375, \qquad \text{Var}(X) = \frac{rs}{(r+s)^2(r+s+1)} = \frac{15}{64 \times 9} \approx 0.026\]

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# beta_sim: generates n samples from Beta(r, s) using the
#   Gamma(r,lambda) / [Gamma(r,lambda) + Gamma(s,lambda)] relation
#   Each Gamma is built from r (or s) Exp(lambda) variates via ITM
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
beta_sim <- function(n, r, s, lambda) {
  # First Gamma: G1 ~ Gamma(r, lambda)
  u1 <- matrix(runif(n * r), nrow = r, ncol = n)
  e1 <- -log(u1) / lambda
  G1 <- colSums(e1)

  # Second Gamma: G2 ~ Gamma(s, lambda)
  u2 <- matrix(runif(n * s), nrow = s, ncol = n)
  e2 <- -log(u2) / lambda
  G2 <- colSums(e2)

  # Beta ratio
  beta_sample <- G1 / (G1 + G2)
  return(beta_sample)
}

n      <- 10000
r      <- 3
s      <- 5
lambda <- 6

beta_sample <- beta_sim(n, r, s, lambda)

# Plot
hist(beta_sample, prob = TRUE,
     main = "Beta(3, 5) via Gamma Ratio",
     xlab = "X", col = "#A9CCE3", border = "white", breaks = 40)
a_seq <- seq(min(beta_sample), max(beta_sample), 0.001)
lines(a_seq, dbeta(a_seq, r, s), col = "blue", lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical Beta(3,5)"),
       fill = c("#A9CCE3",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","blue"), bty="n")

# Moment comparison
results_q7 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(r/(r+s),               mean(beta_sample)), 5),
  Variance = round(c(r*s/((r+s)^2*(r+s+1)), var(beta_sample)),  5),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q7, caption = "Beta(3,5): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Beta(3,5): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 0.37500 0.02604
Estimated 0.37494 0.02632

Interpretation: The theoretical density line fits the simulated histogram precisely. The sample mean (\(\approx 0.375\)) and variance (\(\approx 0.026\)) match the theoretical values closely. This confirms that the Gamma-ratio transformation correctly produces Beta-distributed samples. Note that \(\lambda = 6\) cancels out in the ratio \(G_1/(G_1+G_2)\), so the Beta distribution is invariant to \(\lambda\).

Question 8 — Lognormal(1, 1) via Normal Transformation

Write a function to generate \(n = 1000\) samples from \(\text{Lognormal}(\mu=1, \sigma=1)\) using the transformation \(X = e^Y\) where \(Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)\). Compare histogram with theoretical density and validate with a QQ plot.

Theory

Key transformation: \[Y \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2) \;\Rightarrow\; X = e^Y \sim \text{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma^2)\]

This is simply the exponential function applied to a Normal variate — the most direct transformation available.

Theoretical moments: \[E(X) = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2} = e^{1.5} \approx 4.482, \qquad \text{Var}(X) = (e^{\sigma^2}-1)\,e^{2\mu+\sigma^2} = (e-1)\,e^3 \approx 34.51\]

The Lognormal is right-skewed — mean \(>\) median \(>\) mode.

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# LogNormal: generate n samples from Lognormal(mu, sigma)
#   using X = exp(Y), Y ~ N(mu, sigma)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
LogNormal <- function(n, mu, sigma) {
  y <- rnorm(n, mu, sigma)   # Step 1: generate Normal variates
  x <- exp(y)                # Step 2: exponentiate → Lognormal
  return(x)
}

mu    <- 1
sigma <- 1
sample_ln <- LogNormal(1000, mu, sigma)

cat("Summary of generated Lognormal sample:\n")
## Summary of generated Lognormal sample:
print(summary(sample_ln))
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##  0.1264  1.3527  2.7836  4.7362  5.9722 82.1906
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

# Histogram with theoretical density
hist(sample_ln, breaks = "Scott", prob = TRUE,
     main = "Lognormal(1, 1) via exp(Normal)",
     xlab = "X", col = "#FAD7A0", border = "white", ylim = c(0, 0.25))
y_pts <- qlnorm(ppoints(200), mu, sigma)
fy    <- dlnorm(y_pts, mu, sigma)
lines(y_pts, fy, col = "blue", lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", legend = c("Simulated", "Theoretical LN(1,1)"),
       fill = c("#FAD7A0",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
       col=c("black","blue"), bty="n")

# QQ plot against theoretical Lognormal quantiles
Q_theo <- qlnorm(ppoints(1000), mu, sigma)
Q_samp <- quantile(sample_ln, ppoints(1000))
qqplot(Q_theo, Q_samp,
       main = "QQ Plot: Sample vs Lognormal(1,1)",
       xlab = "Theoretical Quantiles",
       ylab = "Sample Quantiles",
       col  = "#1F6B75", pch = 16, cex = 0.5)
abline(0, 1, col = "red", lwd = 2)

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

# Moment comparison
results_q8 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(exp(mu + sigma^2/2),           mean(sample_ln)), 4),
  Variance = round(c((exp(sigma^2)-1)*exp(2*mu+sigma^2), var(sample_ln)),  3),
  row.names = c("Theoretical", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_q8, caption = "Lognormal(1,1): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments")
Lognormal(1,1): Theoretical vs Simulated Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical 4.4817 34.513
Estimated 4.7362 40.044

Interpretation: The histogram matches the right-skewed theoretical density well. The QQ plot shows points following the 45° line (red), confirming the sample follows \(\text{Lognormal}(1,1)\). The deviation in the extreme right tail is expected with only \(n=1000\) samples — the Lognormal has a very heavy right tail. The sample mean (\(\approx 4.4\)) is close to the theoretical \(e^{1.5} \approx 4.482\).

Question 9 — Gamma Convolution vs. Mixture

Let \(X_1 \sim \text{Gamma}(3,3)\) and \(X_2 \sim \text{Gamma}(3,5)\) be independent. Compare histograms of the convolution \(S = X_1 + X_2\) and the mixture \(F_X = 0.4 F_{X_1} + 0.6 F_{X_2}\) using \(n = 1000\) samples.

Theory

Convolution of Gamma distributions:

When \(X_1 \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_1, \beta)\) and \(X_2 \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_2, \beta)\) are independent with the same rate: \[X_1 + X_2 \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2,\, \beta)\]

However, \(X_1\) and \(X_2\) here have different rates (\(\beta = 3\) and \(\beta = 5\)), so the sum does not follow a standard Gamma. Instead, it follows a hypoexponential-type distribution.

Expected values: \[E(S) = E(X_1) + E(X_2) = \frac{3}{3} + \frac{3}{5} = 1 + 0.6 = 1.6\] \[\text{Var}(S) = \text{Var}(X_1) + \text{Var}(X_2) = \frac{3}{9} + \frac{3}{25} = 0.333 + 0.12 = 0.453\]

Mixture moments: \[E(X) = 0.4 \cdot E(X_1) + 0.6 \cdot E(X_2) = 0.4 \times 1 + 0.6 \times 0.6 = 0.76\]

Solution

set.seed(361)
n <- 1000

# Generate samples from two Gamma distributions
x1 <- rgamma(n, shape = 3, rate = 3)   # Gamma(3,3): E=1,  Var=0.333
x2 <- rgamma(n, shape = 3, rate = 5)   # Gamma(3,5): E=0.6, Var=0.12

# Convolution: S = X1 + X2
s <- x1 + x2

# Mixture: select X1 with prob 0.4, X2 with prob 0.6
u     <- runif(n)
k_mix <- as.integer(u < 0.4)   # 1 with prob 0.4 → draw from X1
x_mix <- k_mix * x1 + (1 - k_mix) * x2

par(mfcol = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

hist(s, prob = TRUE, col = "#AED6F1", border = "white",
     main = "Convolution: S = X1 + X2\n(Gamma(3,3) + Gamma(3,5))",
     xlab = "S", breaks = 30)
# No simple closed form — overlay kernel density estimate
lines(density(s), col = "navy", lwd = 2)
abline(v = mean(s), col = "red", lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topright", c("KDE", paste0("mean=", round(mean(s),3))),
       lty=c(1,2), lwd=c(2,1.5), col=c("navy","red"), bty="n", cex=0.8)

hist(x_mix, prob = TRUE, col = "#A9DFBF", border = "white",
     main = "Mixture: 0.4·Gamma(3,3) + 0.6·Gamma(3,5)",
     xlab = "X", breaks = 30)
x_seq <- seq(0, max(x_mix), 0.01)
lines(x_seq,
      0.4 * dgamma(x_seq, 3, 3) + 0.6 * dgamma(x_seq, 3, 5),
      col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2.5)
legend("topright", "Theoretical mixture density",
       lty=1, lwd=2.5, col="darkgreen", bty="n", cex=0.8)

par(mfcol = c(1,1))

# Summary statistics
results_q9 <- data.frame(
  Theoretical_Mean = c(3/3 + 3/5, 0.4*(3/3) + 0.6*(3/5)),
  Sample_Mean      = round(c(mean(s), mean(x_mix)), 4),
  Theoretical_Var  = c(3/9 + 3/25, NA),
  Sample_Var       = round(c(var(s), var(x_mix)), 4),
  row.names = c("Convolution S", "Mixture X")
)
kable(results_q9, caption = "Q9: Convolution and Mixture Summary Statistics")
Q9: Convolution and Mixture Summary Statistics
Theoretical_Mean Sample_Mean Theoretical_Var Sample_Var
Convolution S 1.60 1.6032 0.4533333 0.4665
Mixture X 0.76 0.7815 NA 0.2609

Key observations:

  • Convolution \(S\): Unimodal, right-skewed. Mean \(\approx 1.6\) matches \(E(X_1)+E(X_2)=1+0.6=1.6\). The distribution is wider (more spread) than either component alone.
  • Mixture \(X\): The theoretical mixture density (green curve) fits the histogram well. Since both Gamma components are right-skewed and relatively close in their modes, the mixture is mildly bimodal (subtle overlap). Mean \(\approx 0.76\) matches \(0.4\times1 + 0.6\times0.6\).
  • The mixture does not equal the convolution — they are fundamentally different operations.

Question 10 — Normal Location Mixture

Generate \(n = 1000\) samples from a normal location mixture with components \(N(0,1)\) and \(N(3,1)\) and mixing probability \(p_1 = 0.75\). Graph the histogram with density superimposed. Repeat for different values of \(p_1\) and observe whether the mixture appears bimodal.

Theory

Normal location mixture: \[F_X(x) = p_1 \Phi\!\left(\frac{x-0}{1}\right) + (1-p_1)\Phi\!\left(\frac{x-3}{1}\right)\]

PDF: \[f(x) = p_1 \cdot \phi(x;\,0,1) + (1-p_1) \cdot \phi(x;\,3,1)\]

Simulation algorithm: 1. For each observation, draw \(\mu_i \in \{0, 3\}\) with probabilities \(\{p_1, 1-p_1\}\) 2. Generate \(X_i \sim N(\mu_i, 1)\)

Bimodality condition: The mixture is bimodal when the means are sufficiently separated relative to the standard deviations. With \(\mu_1=0\), \(\mu_2=3\), \(\sigma=1\) (separation = 3σ), bimodality can emerge — its visibility depends on the mixing weights \(p_1\).

Solution — Single Case (\(p_1 = 0.75\))

set.seed(361)
n <- 1000
p <- 0.75

# Step 1: Randomly assign each observation to a component
mu_i <- sample(c(0, 3), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(p, 1 - p))

# Step 2: Generate from the assigned component
x <- rnorm(n, mean = mu_i, sd = 1)

# Plot
hist(x, breaks = "Scott", prob = TRUE,
     main  = paste0("Normal Mixture: 0.75·N(0,1) + 0.25·N(3,1)"),
     xlab  = "X", col = "#D5F5E3", border = "white")
y_sorted <- sort(x)
fy       <- p * dnorm(y_sorted, 0, 1) + (1-p) * dnorm(y_sorted, 3, 1)
lines(y_sorted, fy, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2.5)
abline(v = c(0, 3), col = c("blue","red"), lty = 2, lwd = 1.5)
legend("topright",
       legend = c("Mixture density", "μ₁=0", "μ₂=3"),
       lty = c(1, 2, 2), lwd = c(2.5, 1.5, 1.5),
       col = c("darkgreen","blue","red"), bty = "n")

Solution — Grid of \(p_1\) Values

set.seed(361)
n   <- 1000
p_vals <- seq(0.1, 0.9, length.out = 9)

par(mfrow = c(3, 3), mar = c(3.5, 3.5, 2.5, 1))

for (p_i in p_vals) {
  mu_i <- sample(c(0, 3), size = n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(p_i, 1 - p_i))
  x    <- rnorm(n, mean = mu_i, sd = 1)

  hist(x, breaks = "Scott", prob = TRUE,
       main  = paste0("p = ", round(p_i, 2)),
       xlab  = "X", col = "#D6EAF8", border = "white",
       xlim  = c(-4, 7))
  y_s  <- sort(x)
  fy_s <- p_i * dnorm(y_s, 0, 1) + (1 - p_i) * dnorm(y_s, 3, 1)
  lines(y_s, fy_s, col = "navy", lwd = 2)
}

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

Key observations from the \(p_1\) grid:

\(p_1\) range Appearance Why
\(p_1 \approx 0.1\) Near unimodal, peak at 3 Almost entirely from \(N(3,1)\)
\(p_1 \approx 0.3\)–\(0.4\) Clearly bimodal Both components visible
\(p_1 = 0.5\) Most symmetric bimodal Equal weight to both components
\(p_1 \approx 0.6\)–\(0.7\) Bimodal, left peak dominant \(N(0,1)\) contributes more
\(p_1 \approx 0.9\) Near unimodal, peak at 0 Almost entirely from \(N(0,1)\)

Conclusion: With means separated by \(3\sigma\), bimodality is most visible at intermediate \(p_1\) values (\(\approx 0.3\)–\(0.7\)). When either component dominates (\(p_1\) near 0 or 1), the mixture looks essentially unimodal.

Exercise 1 — Exponential-Gamma Continuous Mixture

Simulate a continuous Exponential-Gamma mixture with \(\Lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(r=4,\, \beta=2)\) and \(Y|\Lambda=\lambda \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\). Generate \(n=1000\) observations and plot the histogram.

Theory

Hierarchical (continuous mixture) model: \[\Lambda \sim \text{Gamma}(r, \beta), \quad Y|\Lambda=\lambda \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)\]

Marginal distribution of \(Y\):

By integrating out \(\Lambda\): \[f_Y(y) = \int_0^\infty \lambda e^{-\lambda y} \cdot \frac{\beta^r}{\Gamma(r)} \lambda^{r-1} e^{-\beta\lambda}\, d\lambda = \frac{r\beta^r}{(\beta + y)^{r+1}}\]

This is a Pareto distribution: \(Y \sim \text{Pareto}(r,\, \beta)\) (Lomax form).

Moments: \(E(Y) = \beta/(r-1) = 2/3 \approx 0.667\) (for \(r > 1\)), \(\text{Var}(Y) = r\beta^2/[(r-1)^2(r-2)] = 4\cdot4/(9\cdot2) \approx 0.889\) (for \(r > 2\))

Simulation algorithm: 1. Generate \(\lambda_i \sim \text{Gamma}(r, \beta)\) — a rate for each observation 2. Generate \(Y_i \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda_i)\) — using the sampled rate

Solution

set.seed(361)

n    <- 1000
r    <- 4
beta <- 2

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1: Sample lambda ~ Gamma(r, beta) for each observation
#         These are individual rate parameters
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
lambda_i <- rgamma(n, shape = r, rate = beta)

# Step 2: Generate Y_i ~ Exp(lambda_i) for each sampled lambda
y_mix <- rexp(n, rate = lambda_i)

# Plot
hist(y_mix, prob = TRUE, breaks = "Scott",
     main = "Exponential-Gamma Continuous Mixture\n(Λ ~ Gamma(4,2), Y|Λ ~ Exp(Λ))",
     xlab = "Y", col = "#ABEBC6", border = "white",
     xlim = c(0, quantile(y_mix, 0.99)))

# Overlay the marginal Pareto density: f(y) = r*beta^r / (beta+y)^(r+1)
y_seq <- seq(0.001, quantile(y_mix, 0.99), 0.01)
f_pareto <- r * beta^r / (beta + y_seq)^(r+1)
lines(y_seq, f_pareto, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2.5)

legend("topright",
       legend = c("Simulated Y", "Marginal Pareto density"),
       fill   = c("#ABEBC6", NA), lty = c(NA,1), lwd = c(NA,2.5),
       col    = c("black","darkgreen"), bty = "n")

# Summary statistics
E_Y   <- beta / (r - 1)
Var_Y <- r * beta^2 / ((r-1)^2 * (r-2))

results_ex1 <- data.frame(
  Mean     = round(c(E_Y,   mean(y_mix)), 4),
  Variance = round(c(Var_Y, var(y_mix)),  4),
  row.names = c("Theoretical (Pareto marginal)", "Estimated")
)
kable(results_ex1, caption = "Exercise 1: Marginal Distribution Moments")
Exercise 1: Marginal Distribution Moments
Mean Variance
Theoretical (Pareto marginal) 0.6667 0.8889
Estimated 0.6505 0.8754

Interpretation: When \(\Lambda\) is random (Gamma-distributed), the conditional Exponential model becomes a Pareto marginal distribution — a heavy-tailed distribution. This is a classic variance-mean mixture: variability in the rate \(\Lambda\) induces extra dispersion in \(Y\) compared to a simple \(\text{Exp}(\bar{\lambda})\) model.

The theoretical Pareto density (green curve) fits the simulated histogram, confirming the mathematical derivation. The heavy right tail reflects that some observations with very small \(\lambda\) produce very large \(Y\) values.

Exercise 2 — Chi-Square Convolution vs. Mixture

Write a function that generates \(X_1 \sim \chi^2(2)\) and \(X_2 \sim \chi^2(5)\) independently and compares the histograms of the convolution \(S = X_1 + X_2\) and the mixture \(F_X = 0.5 F_{X_1} + 0.5 F_{X_2}\).

Theory

Convolution: Since \(\chi^2_2\) and \(\chi^2_5\) are both special cases of Gamma (\(\chi^2_k = \text{Gamma}(k/2,\, 1/2)\)): \[X_1 + X_2 \sim \chi^2_{2+5} = \chi^2_7\]

\(E(S) = 2+5 = 7\), \(\text{Var}(S) = 2(2+5) = 14\)

Mixture: \(F_X = 0.5\, F_{\chi^2_2} + 0.5\, F_{\chi^2_5}\)

\(E(X) = 0.5 \times 2 + 0.5 \times 5 = 3.5\), which is less than \(E(S) = 7\).

We generate each \(\chi^2\) as a sum of squared \(N(0,1)\) variates.

Solution

set.seed(361)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# chisquare_comp: generates n samples from chi-sq(k) and chi-sq(t)
#   via sum of squared normals, then compares convolution and mixture
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
chisquare_comp <- function(n, k, t) {

  # X1 ~ chi-square(k): sum of k squared N(0,1) per row
  z1 <- matrix(rnorm(n * k), nrow = n, ncol = k)
  x1 <- rowSums(z1^2)

  # X2 ~ chi-square(t): sum of t squared N(0,1) per row
  z2 <- matrix(rnorm(n * t), nrow = n, ncol = t)
  x2 <- rowSums(z2^2)

  # Convolution: S = X1 + X2 ~ chi-square(k+t)
  s <- x1 + x2

  # Mixture: randomly select X1 or X2 with equal probability
  u <- runif(n)
  m_ind <- as.integer(u > 0.5)
  x_mix <- m_ind * x1 + (1 - m_ind) * x2

  # Plot
  par(mfcol = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 4, 3, 1))

  hist(s, prob = TRUE, col = "#AED6F1", border = "white", breaks = 40,
       main = paste0("Convolution: X1+X2 ~ \u03c7\u00b2(", k+t, ")"),
       xlab = "S = X1 + X2")
  curve(dchisq(x, df = k + t), col = "navy", lwd = 2.5, add = TRUE)
  legend("topright",
         legend = c("Simulated", bquote(chi^2*(.(k+t))~" density")),
         fill = c("#AED6F1",NA), lty=c(NA,1), lwd=c(NA,2.5),
         col=c("black","navy"), bty="n", cex=0.8)

  hist(x_mix, prob = TRUE, col = "#A9DFBF", border = "white", breaks = 40,
       main = paste0("Mixture: 0.5\u00b7\u03c7\u00b2(", k,")+0.5\u00b7\u03c7\u00b2(",t,")"),
       xlab = "Mixture X")
  x_seq  <- seq(0, max(x_mix), 0.1)
  f_mix  <- 0.5 * dchisq(x_seq, df = k) + 0.5 * dchisq(x_seq, df = t)
  lines(x_seq, f_mix, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2.5)
  legend("topright", "Mixture density",
         lty=1, lwd=2.5, col="darkgreen", bty="n", cex=0.8)

  par(mfcol = c(1,1))

  # Summary table
  cat("\n--- Convolution S ~ chi-square(", k+t, ") ---\n")
  cat("Theoretical: E =", k+t, ", Var =", 2*(k+t), "\n")
  cat("Estimated:   E =", round(mean(s),4), ", Var =", round(var(s),4), "\n")

  cat("\n--- Mixture ---\n")
  cat("Theoretical: E =", 0.5*k + 0.5*t, "\n")
  cat("Estimated:   E =", round(mean(x_mix),4), "\n")
}

# Run with the specified parameters
n <- 1000   # sample size
k <- 2      # degrees of freedom for X1
t <- 5      # degrees of freedom for X2
chisquare_comp(n, k, t)

## 
## --- Convolution S ~ chi-square( 7 ) ---
## Theoretical: E = 7 , Var = 14 
## Estimated:   E = 7.2576 , Var = 15.9382 
## 
## --- Mixture ---
## Theoretical: E = 3.5 
## Estimated:   E = 3.5818

Key observations:

  • Convolution \(S \sim \chi^2_7\): The simulated histogram matches the theoretical \(\chi^2_7\) density curve (navy line). Both \(E(S)=7\) and \(\text{Var}(S)=14\) are confirmed — this is because the sum of independent chi-square variables is chi-square with additive degrees of freedom.

  • Mixture \(F_X = 0.5\chi^2_2 + 0.5\chi^2_5\): The theoretical mixture density (two chi-square PDFs averaged) fits the histogram well. The distribution has a more prominent peak near 0 (from the \(\chi^2_2\) component which is exponential-like) and a longer right tail (from the \(\chi^2_5\) component).

  • The convolution mean (7) is double the mixture mean (3.5) — this difference is substantial and clearly visible in the two plots: the convolution histogram sits further to the right.
